CitedMedium="Internet"><PubDate><Year>2026</Year><Month>Jul</Month><Day>13</Day></PubDate></JournalIssue><Title>Otology & neurotology : official publication of the American Otological Society, American Neurotology Society [and] European Academy of Otology and Neurotology</Title><ISOAbbreviation>Otol Neurotol</ISOAbbreviation></Journal><ArticleTitle>Predictive Validity of Hypothesized Risk Factors for Superior Canal Dehiscence Syndrome.</ArticleTitle><ELocationID EIdType="doi" ValidYN="Y">10.1097/MAO.0000000000005007</ELocationID><Abstract><AbstractText Label="OBJECTIVE" NlmCategory="OBJECTIVE">To assess the predictive validity of comorbid conditions hypothesized to be risk factors of superior canal dehiscence syndrome (SCDS).</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="STUDY DESIGN" NlmCategory="METHODS">Retrospective case-control study.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="SETTING" NlmCategory="METHODS">A metropolitan health care system.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="PATIENTS" NlmCategory="METHODS">Adult patients with superior canal dehiscence syndrome (n=152) and matched controls (n=438) between the years 2016 and 2021.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="INTERVENTION" NlmCategory="METHODS">Diagnostic.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="MAIN OUTCOME MEASURES" NlmCategory="METHODS">Differences in rates of comorbid diagnoses evaluated by multivariate logistic regression: disorders of bone density (osteoporosis, osteopenia, or osteomalacia), vitamin D deficiency, hypocalcemia, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), idiopathic intracranial hypertension, history of head trauma, and BMI >25.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="RESULTS" NlmCategory="RESULTS">The study patients had a mean age of 57.8 years, with 62.5% females. Two diagnoses achieved significance (P<0.05) in the multivariate logistic regression: OSA was a positive predictor of SCDS with an OR of 2.29 (95% CI: 1.15-4.54), whereas having a BMI over 25 was a negative predictor with an OR of 0.51 (95% CI: 0.29-0.89). The remaining diagnoses either did not achieve a statistically significant association or were excluded from multivariate analysis due to rarity in the cohort.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="CONCLUSIONS" NlmCategory="CONCLUSIONS">OSA was a significant positive predictor of SCDS while elevated BMI acted as a negative predictor, suggesting that comorbid OSA may confound previously reported links between elevated BMI and SCDS.</AbstractText><CopyrightInformation>Copyright © 2026, Otology & Neurotology, Inc.</CopyrightInformation></Abstract><AuthorList CompleteYN="Y"><Author
